statement: When nc(B)=1 for all B in [1,256], itl(B) is exactly affine in B with slope delta=(w_prefill(1)+w_decode)/(1+m), and the ITL-binding M can be computed in closed form: M_ITL=clamp(floor(1+(targetITL-itl(1))/delta),1,256), matching a brute scan with zero discrepancy.
regime: Dev-set profiles where AvgInputTokens+AvgOutputTokens<<MaxNumTokens=8192, so nc stays at 1 throughout [1,256]. All six dev scenarios satisfy this condition.
mechanism: With nc=1, formulas.py:69-73 computes delta once (independent of B). bg(B)=alpha+(B-1)*delta (formulas.py:81-83) is exactly linear; itl(B)=bg(B)+beta+gamma*(n+(m+1)/2) is exactly affine (formulas.py:93-96). Inverting an affine integer inequality is exact, so floor-form == brute scan.
applicability_bounds: Holds only when nc(B)=1 across the entire batch range. Breaks for high-input profiles where nc jumps (see RP-4). The dev set is entirely in the nc=1 regime; the benchmark grid is not.

statement: M_ITL is a lower bound on the oracle plateau-onset M*, not M* itself. Realized AvgITL averages over occupancy <=B, so AvgITL<=itl(B); the ITL SLO can be satisfied at batch sizes above M_ITL. The gap (M_truth/M_ITL ≈ 1.6–2.8 on ITL-binding scenarios) must be closed by an occupancy-gap correction derived from the queue solver.
regime: All constraint-binding scenarios (ITL-binding or crossover regime). The unbounded regime is exempt (M_ITL=M_truth=256 in that case).
mechanism: itl(B) is the full-batch iteration time (all B slots occupied). The M/M/1 queue solver's realized AvgITL is a weighted average over occupancy 1..B, which is strictly less than itl(B) whenever occupancy<B has positive probability. Therefore itl(M_ITL)=targetITL but AvgITL(M_ITL,RPS)<targetITL, meaning larger batches are still feasible.
applicability_bounds: The bound direction (M_ITL <= M*) is universal under nc=1. The magnitude of the gap depends on the traffic regime (RPS relative to saturation). Iter-3 will quantify the gap via oracle validation.

statement: Saturation throughput S(B)=1000*B/tau(B) is monotonically increasing and concave in B under nc=1, approaching the analytic ceiling S_inf=1000/((1+m)*delta) from below (ratio 0.75–0.96 at B=256 on dev scenarios). Throughput is non-decreasing in M, so the useful upper bound on M comes from the binding latency constraint, not from saturation.
regime: nc=1 regime (dev set). Concavity implies diminishing returns but no interior maximum. May require piecewise analysis for nc>1 profiles where tau has slope changes.
mechanism: With nc=1, tau(B)=(1+m)*(alpha+B*delta) (formulas.py:76-79). S(B)=1000/(1+m) * B/(alpha+B*delta). The function x/(a+bx) is increasing and concave for a,b>0, with limit 1/b. Hence S_inf=1000/((1+m)*delta). No interior maximum exists, so increasing M never hurts throughput.
applicability_bounds: Holds strictly under nc=1. For nc>1, tau has piecewise-linear slopes and S may have local non-monotone segments near nc jumps. Not validated on the benchmark grid.

statement: nc=1 is a NECESSARY condition for the ITL closed form to exactly match the brute scan. When nc(B) jumps within [1,256], delta changes at the jump and itl becomes piecewise-affine; a single-slope inversion from B=1 misestimates the crossing by up to one integer step.
regime: High-input profiles where n*nc > MaxNumTokens=8192 at some batch size within [1,256]. For the ablation profile (n=7500, m=64), nc jumps at B=6,39,51,57,62,65,67,70 and nc_max=174.
mechanism: formulas.py:69-73 recomputes nc(B) per call to delta(B). When nc increases at a batch-size jump, w_prefill(nc) grows and delta(B) increases above the nc-1 value. An affine inversion using delta(1) (nc=1 slope) underestimates the crossing batch size because the true itl curve bends upward at the jump, reaching targetITL sooner than the extrapolated single-slope line predicts.
applicability_bounds: The mismatch is bounded (observed: 1 integer step for the ablation profile). For a piecewise-correct closed form, delta must be recomputed per nc-segment. The benchmark grid (nous/scenarios_benchmark.json) likely contains many nc>1 profiles and will need this piecewise treatment.

statement: The prefill-only TTFT crossing M_TTFT_prefill=clamp(floor(1+(targetTTFT-ttft_prefill(1))/delta),1,256) is an UPPER bound on the true TTFT-binding M, because full TTFT=ttft_prefill(B)+queue_wait and the queue solver adds a positive wait term. Increasing M raises TTFT above the primitive-only estimate.
regime: TTFT-binding and crossover scenarios. Observed M_TTFT_prefill values: ttft-only=70, alpha-high=45, baseline=147. Not directly gate-tested in iter-1 (the TTFT gates were not included in the validator); inferred from structural argument and per-scenario M_TTFT_prefill_closed_form values in baseline.json.
mechanism: ttft_prefill(B) is the batch-time component of TTFT (formulas.py:86-89). The M/M/1 solver adds queue_wait >= 0 to get the realized AvgTTFT. Hence AvgTTFT >= ttft_prefill(B), and the M where ttft_prefill(B)=targetTTFT is a batch size that already violates the full TTFT SLO when queue wait is non-trivial.
applicability_bounds: The upper-bound direction is universal (queue_wait >= 0 always). Tightness depends on the traffic load: at low RPS the wait is negligible and M_TTFT_prefill is a tight upper bound; near saturation the wait is large and M_TTFT_prefill over-estimates M*_TTFT significantly. Quantifying the gap is iter-2/3 work.
